Root Cause of Delay

observations.managementAction:
The Management had directed AAO to ensure strict compliance with Section 56 of RA 11975 by closely monitoring payroll deductions; to formulate internal policies and procedures governing the evaluation and approval of employees’ salary loan applications to be submitted to the Municipal Mayor for review and approval; and to refrain from issuing or signing certifications for loan applications in cases where an employee’s NTHP would fall below the minimum threshold of P5,000.00, exclusive of other loan obligations.
Also, the Management directed the Human Resource and Development Officer to conduct extensive information and awareness campaigns for all employees to promote awareness and knowledge about loan-related policies and requirements.
The AAO and HRMO will work closely together to enforce such policies and to strictly comply with existing laws, rules and regulations.
observations.reason:
There are still seven (7) employees receiving NTHP below P5,000.00 as of August 2025. These employees had applied with their loans prior to the receipt of AOM. After receiving the AOM, the AAO and HRMO strictly implemented the recommendation of closely monitoring the payroll deductions to avoid additional employees availing loans resulting to a NTHP below P5,000.00. | The Municipality is anticipating the salary increase for the second tranche this September 2025. If implemented, almost all the employees with monthly NTHP below the mandated minimum amount of P5,000.00 will all be cleared. Based form the list attached from the HRMO, there will be two (2) remaining employees with NTHP below P5,000.00 amounting to P4,968.91 and P4,520.62 respectively
(see attached list from the HRMO).

A certification is also being secured before the approval of loans to ensure that the employee availing a loan will still have a NTHP not lower than the minimum of ₱5,000.00

The HRMO will conduct extensive information and awareness campaigns for all employees before the end of the year.

The AAO and HRMO will work closely together to enforce such policies and to strictly comply with existing laws, rules and regulations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to:

Provide Detailed Estimates or DUPA of the lump sum work item of Billboard in the preparation of ABC and POW of the Municipality’s Infrastructure Projects; and

In the ensuing years, submit photos of signboard showing compliance with the design and format particularly project status of infrastructure projects provided under COA Circular No. 2013-004.
observations.managementAction:
Detailed Estimates: We will ensure that detailed estimates or the Detailed Unit Price Analysis (DUPA) for all lump-sum work items, including billboards, are provided in the preparation of the ABC and POW for our infrastructure projects. 

Enhanced Documentation: We will enhance our documentation processes to include all necessary breakdowns and justifications for each component of the lump-sum work items, ensuring full compliance with the COA's requirements. 

Photographic Documentation: For all future infrastructure projects, we will submit photos of the signboards that clearly show compliance with the prescribed design and format, including the current status of the project. 
Ongoing Monitoring: We will implement regular monitoring and auditing procedures to ensure continuous compliance with these requirements throughout the duration of each project. 

Training and Awareness: Conduct training sessions for project managers and relevant personnel to ensure they are fully aware of the design, format, and information requirements for project signboards as stipulated by COA Circular No. 2013-004.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, direct the Project Engineers to:

Strictly require all contractors to ensure their entire workforce is wearing the required safety gear or personal protective equipment at all times within the construction project site for the protection and welfare of both the construction workers and the general public; 

Closely monitor the proper implementation of the CSHP and Standards by the contractors at the project sites by maintaining detailed records of contractor’s non-compliance per site visitation, which can be referenced during future payment processes; and

Formulate an internal policy that clearly defines the appropriate sanctions or penalties to be applied to contractors found in violation of the CSHP to ensure accountability and adherence to safety protocols.
observations.managementAction:
Strict Enforcement: We will strictly require all contractors to ensure their entire workforce is wearing the required safety gear or PPE at all times within the construction project site.

Regular Inspections: Conduct regular inspections to ensure compliance with PPE requirements. Any instances of non-compliance will be documented and addressed promptly. 

Detailed Records: Maintain detailed records of each contractor's compliance with CSHP requirements during site visits. These records will document any instances of non-compliance and corrective actions taken. 

Reference for Payments: Use the records of non-compliance as a reference during future payment processes to ensure that contractors adhere to safety standards before receiving full payment. 

Internal Policy Formulation: Formulate an internal policy that clearly defines appropriate sanctions or penalties for contractors found in violation of the CSHP. This policy will outline specific penalties for various types of non-compliance to ensure accountability and adherence to safety protocols.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to:

Require personnel who undertake inspection or assessment to make a monitoring, inspection, or accomplishment report for every site inspection, which must be duly documented and backed-up with photos; and

Defer the recommendation of suspension of work in the absence of proofs to back-up reasons stated therein or those not in accordance with RA No. 9184. In the absence of supporting documents, the unworkable days perceived in the PAG-ASA Reports should be controlled in the grant of time extensions.
observations.managementAction:
Standardized Reporting Templates: Development and distribution of standardized templates for all types of inspections and assessments.

Documentation and Back-up: Mandatory inclusion of detailed documentation and photographic evidence including a certification from the Barangay Captain of the project location to substantiate the findings of each report. 
Documentation Requirement: Any recommendation for work suspension will only be considered if it is accompanied by comprehensive documentation and evidence that aligns with RA No. 9184.

Verification Process: Establish a verification process to review and validate the provided documents and evidence before making any decisions on work suspension.

Control of Unworkable Days Based on PAG-ASA Reports: Unworkable days, as perceived from PAG-ASA weather reports, should be carefully controlled when granting time extensions.

We recommended that the LCE:
Conduct thorough evaluation on the status of the project, and if still possible, come-up with concrete plans that would remedy or salvage the project in order to avoid wastage/loss of government funds;
Require the Municipal Planning and Development Office (MPDO) and Engineering Office to judiciously and meticulously plan, conduct actual and thorough site inspection and evaluation of every proposed project before preparing the design plans of the project. The detailed engineering investigation and design must be sufficiently carried out taking into consideration all the scope of work needed so that the purpose and benefits for which a project is constructed could be attained; and
Conduct a feasibility study which serves as a basis for the design parameter of an infrastructure project and consultation with the residents of the community before proceeding to the activities on detailed engineering.
Management commented in the exit conference that the on-going construction of Reinforced Concrete Box Culvert along Barangay Balbalayang Road has been made public with competent awareness especially among the Balbalayang Barangay Officials during the planning and evaluation stage early on before the design plans were made.
Further, during the initial study – on-site, measurement works as preparatory to design concept, the Barangay Officials and members of the community at large were present with several of them coming up with suggestions and recommendations which were considered in the final design concept.
Moreover, during the initial study – on-site, measurement works as preparatory to design concept, the Barangay Officials and members of the community at large were present with several of them coming up with suggestions and recommendations which were considered in the final design concept.
Moreover, with the funding source identified, the planned project construction was presented, discussed and deliberated during the Local Development Council (LDC) meeting regularly convened as mandated for their approval of Municipal projects. In the LDC meeting, all identified project for implementation are discussed and approved – all Barangay Officials and accredited Non-Government Organizations (NGOs) and other stakeholders present to give signatures of approval and consensus.
Additionally, the LDC crafted resolution approving these projects and its implementation passes thru the Sanggunian Bayan which gave their approval on the said LDC Resolution – this project Reinforced Concrete Box Culvert in Barangay Balbalayang included.
With regard to the design and scope of works, several factors were considered such as the realization that bigger pre-fabricated I-beams (an alternate design plan) and other bigger construction materials including equipment and difficulty of transport vehicles (cranes/boom) will not be able to reach the project site. These factors, among others, as part of the study that led to the final design concept which were all regularly made known and presented to the Barangay Officials and community members of Barangay Balbalayang.
Moreover, as to the scope and schedule of works implementation, included program of works is a slope protection as additional protective barrier to divert massive flood water in case of river – swelling due to increase in volume during typhoons and incessant heavy rains. The construction of the slope protection component chronologically identified in the schedules work implementation prior to the construction of the box culvert may have averted and directed the massive water flowing from the river if the slope protection was first conducted.
Additionally, Typhoon “Maring” is one of the highly – unusual rain – drenching typhoons we have. Proof of its devastation and effect have in La Union are the towns of Luna, Bangar and other municipalities being submerged or have not this kind of experience of wide-ranging devastation. In the municipality, many infrastructures were destroyed which have withstood typhoons of years past, several houses collapsed due to the volume of water in the critical hours of continuous heavy rains.
The situation in Barangay Balbalayang and nearby Barangays have not been spared of this situation, the volume of water was unexpected with debris trapped in the constructed box culvert which hindered free and easier river water passage away from the land mass where some houses are located and nearly reached by the water level. But the erected structure part of the box culvert stands unscathed and undamaged. It might have helped in preventing a big fallen log carried by the river stream avert from going to the nearby houses and caused even greater potential property damaged and possible loss of life. The schedule of works if timely followed is a possible deterrent to redirect rampaging waters if earlier constructed.
With the petition of the community, the Punong Barangay had been updated and a community meeting is scheduled on June 17, 2022 to hear-out and finalized the request to lower the height of the planned culvert.

observations.recommendation: For the proper management and handling of the PPE, we recommended that management conduct the required physical count of the PPE as of December 31 of every year. The actual count may be undertaken any month during the current year, but has to be updated at year-end. This procedure is a must in order to know the presence/physical existence of the fixed assets and their conditions; and to identify, segregate and reclassify unserviceable assets. Reconciliation of the result of the count with the accounting records is to be undertaken to effect the necessary adjustments; and to pinpoint responsibility/accountability/liability for missing PPEs. Moreover, to maintain/update the subsidiary ledgers, PPE ledger cards, AREs and placing of property tags which are required under MNGAS for proper control and monitoring of the PPEs and for facility in identifying them.

We recommended that management enjoin the Inventory Committee to include in the conduct of inventory of the properties of the municipality the infrastructures and other community facilities and for the Inventory Committee to prepare and submit the required Report to the DILG. We recommended further that the Municipal Accountant complete the Registry of Public Infrastructure and disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit intact all her collections as well as all collections turned over to her by the collectors/tellers with the authorized depository bank daily or not later than the next banking day.  We further recommended that she shall record all deposits made in the cashbook and prepare the RCD.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, as the Internal Auditor and one who certifies the validity of the transactions and completeness of supporting documents, as well as the Municipal Treasurer, who certifies the funds availability, to comply with Fundamental Principles for Disbursement of Public Funds as provided under Section 4 of P.D. No 1445. We further recommended that deficiencies noted on the foregoing transactions be addressed with and submitted to the Audit Team in order to ascertain the legality, propriety and validity of the transactions.

We recommended that management require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the following journal entry to recognize completed projects under BUB and ADM as assets in the books of the agency in compliance with PPSAS Nos. 1 and 17 and DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-3 dated May 23, 2017 for fair presentation of the financial statements, to wit:
Road Network					8,188,678.43
Building						   811,000.00
Water System					1,947,114.37
Machineries and Equipment		   715,000.00
Government Equity					11,661,792.80

We recommended that Management plan and calendar the implementation of the programs and projects under the 20% Development Fund. We recommended further that the Municipality avoid the practice of bidding them out at the end of the year since funding source is immediately set aside upon receipt of the IRA.

We recommended that management cause the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant to transfer for deposit the fund to the LDRRMF account solely for disaster risk reduction and management activities in the Trust Fund books in compliance with COA Circular 2012-02.

We recommended that Management appropriate in the Annual Budget at least one percent (1%) of their IRA for LCPC programs, projects and activities as required under Section 15 of RA 9344. We recommended further that the design of PPAs should be in accordance with the enumeration under Section II of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.

We recommended that Management, thru its MDRRMC study and evaluate its Annual MDRRM Plan and determine constraints/reasons for the minimal implementation of its plans and programs, especially the Mitigation Fund, resulting in the failure to attain its intended objectives and non-compliance to disaster preparedness minimum standards for DRRM.

We recommended that Municipal Treasurer properly maintain the Cashbooks, reconcile it with the accounting records and to record cash transactions therein in accordance with Sections 26 to 28 of Vol. II MNGAS for LGUs in order to monitor balances at any given time.

We recommended that management adopt measures that would facilitate the submission of their accounts on time as prescribed in the foregoing Sections of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009 and P.D. 1445. We further recommended that additional staff with accounting background be assigned at the Accounting Office in order to address the delay on the submission of accounts.

We recommended that Management cause strict compliance to COA Circular 2009-1 on the timely submission of contracts with all the supporting documents by the BAC Chairperson/Secretariat to the Auditor within five (5) days upon execution/issuance of the contracts.

We recommended that the MT prepare and submit regularly the monthly Consolidated Report of Accountability for Accountable Forms in order to establish the accountability of accountable forms at any given time.

For the proper management and handling of the PPE, we have recommended that management conduct the required complete physical count of the PPE as of December 31 of every year. The actual count may be undertaken any month during the current year, but has to be updated at year-end. This procedure is a must in order to know the presence/physical existence of the fixed assets and their conditions. We further recommended that the General Service Officer and the Municipal Accountant maintain/update and reconcile their stock card/property cards and place property tags which are required under MNGAS for proper control and monitoring of the PPEs and for facility in identifying them. We recommended likewise that the inventory team pursue the valuation of the Municipal Building and School Buildings in order that discrepancies between book and property records be cleared immediately and submit the reconciled Physical Inventory Report not later than January 31 of each year to avoid accumulation of unreconciled balance of PPE accounts.
